title: Chip-carved sycamore trinket box lid
subtitle: A geometric chip-carved panel for a small box lid in seasoned sycamore
excerpt: Chip carving reduces a flat panel to a pattern of triangular cuts; the three-stroke chip is the foundation of European folk decoration. This lid is a 12 by 8 cm sycamore panel with a border of triangles and a six-pointed star at the centre. A project for an afternoon at the bench; the shadows the cuts throw deepen as the light changes.
sourceNotes: Central European chip-carving tradition, documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885); six-pointed star grid geometry from Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content for indoor use.
A wide flat blade ground to a 65-degree point, held with the thumb on the spine for making precise angled cuts into a flat panel.
A vertical cut into the wood that defines the boundary of a chip and prevents the grain from tearing past it.

content.text: Chip carving is the practice of removing small triangular pieces from a flat panel with two or three knife strokes; each chip a valley, each group of valleys a pattern. Sycamore is the standard European chip-carving wood: pale, close-grained, and
seasoned wood
that holds a sharp edge without tearing. This lid project teaches the three-stroke chip on a border of triangles before moving to the six-pointed star at the centre.
The wood

A panel of kiln-dried sycamore: 12 cm long, 8 cm wide, 0.8 cm thick. Flat-sawn stock shows the face grain more clearly than quarter-sawn for chip-carved patterns. The grain must run straight along the length; any deviation causes the chip wall on one side to tear rather than cut cleanly. Lime (basswood) works identically and is slightly softer, making it the better choice for a first chip-carving attempt.
Tools
A
chip-carving knife

for the triangle cuts, a marking knife for scoring the layout lines before cutting, and a leather strop. A 2H pencil for the grid; hard enough to make a fine line, soft enough to sand off. Danish oil for finishing.
Design

The border pattern: a row of equilateral triangles 8 mm on each side, base against the panel edge, running around the perimeter. Lay this out with a ruler and 60-degree set square. The star: a 5 cm diameter circle at the panel centre, divided into 12 equal sectors. Each pair of adjacent sectors forms a diamond; alternating diamonds are chipped, creating the six-pointed star. Draw the full design in pencil and mark each triangle to be removed with an X before cutting anything.
Method
Each triangle chip uses two
stop cuts
and one release cut. Strop every 20 minutes; chip carving dulls the edge quickly and a dull knife tears the grain instead of slicing it.

Score the boundary of each triangle to be removed with the marking knife: a light score, 0.5 mm deep, on all three sides. This sets the wall before the chip knife works the interior and prevents tear-out at the edges.

Cut each border triangle with the chip-carving knife. First cut: hold the knife at 65 degrees to the surface, point at the triangle apex, and draw the blade along one long side of the triangle toward the base. Second cut: the same angle on the opposite long side, also toward the base. These two cuts meet at depth to form a V-valley. Third cut: the release cut along the triangle base, angled to undercut the chip. The chip lifts free as a single piece.

Work the border triangles in full rows around the perimeter before moving to the star. The star diamonds are narrower and the cuts must be shallower at the centre point; take smaller passes and check depth against the border triangles to keep the shadow depth consistent.
Keep the knife angle consistent across all chips. Variation in angle makes the shadow depth inconsistent and the pattern loses clarity when lit from the side.
Sand the flat panel faces lightly with 180 grit to remove pencil marks. Do not sand into the chip valleys; the knife-cut walls should remain unpolished so the shadow contrast is sharp.
Finishing

Apply one coat of Danish oil with a cloth, working it into the chip valleys. Let it soak for 10 minutes, then wipe off the excess. Danish oil sets in 24 hours. Do not use wax; wax fills the chip valley corners and reduces the shadow depth the pattern depends on.
Variations
Lime (basswood): softer than sycamore, cuts more easily. The better choice for a first chip-carving attempt.
Rosette instead of star: 8 or 12 equal triangular chips arranged around a central point, no outer border. Simpler to lay out.
Border only, no star: a single row of border triangles framing a plain centre. Good for a first panel before attempting the star geometry.
Care
Dust the chip valleys with a dry cloth. Re-oil annually. Keep away from prolonged moisture; the chip walls are cross-grain in texture and absorb water readily.

items.symptom: Chip wall tears rather than cuts cleanly
items.cause: Knife too dull, or cutting against the grain on one side
items.fix: Strop the knife before each row of chips. If tearing persists on one side of a cut, try cutting that side from the opposite direction.

items.symptom: Chips do not release cleanly and break rather than lifting free
items.cause: Release cut not deep enough or angled too shallowly
items.fix: Deepen the release cut by 1 mm and angle it 5 degrees more steeply toward the base of the chip. The chip should lift free with the third stroke.

items.symptom: Shadow depth varies across the pattern
items.fix: Maintain the same 65-degree angle throughout. Mark the angle on a scrap piece and check the knife position against it before starting each new row.
